user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  Total assets are essentially flat, oscillating in a narrow band around $4.48-4.50T with no clear trend. The 4-week change (-$4.2B) and 13-week change (-$7.0B) are both tiny relative to the ~$4.49T balance sheet level (13w % change just -0.16%), and the series shows noisy up-and-down moves (e.g., 4501695 then 4488840 then 4482349) rather than a sustained directional slope. This is consistent with the policy language, which describes reinvestment of principal payments from agency debt/MBS into agency MBS and rolling over maturing Treasuries at auction — i.e., a reinvestment (roughly balance-sheet-maintaining) policy rather than an announced runoff cap or active QE purchase program. There is no mention of tapering reinvestment or allowing runoff, so the modest recent decline looks like normal auction/settlement noise around a flat reinvestment policy rather than the start of a new QT pace. My prior view (if it called flat) is supported by this data; the small negative drift is not large enough to call outright runoff. Absent an announced change in reinvestment policy, I expect month-end total assets to be little changed from the latest reading, within a small band of noise.
Would change its mind if: A statement announcing an end to reinvestment or an explicit runoff cap, or several consecutive weeks of assets declining beyond noise (>$20-30B/month), would flip this to down.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_13w_avg

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series shows a clear, persistent uptrend: from -0.47 thirteen weeks ago to -0.28 now, with 4-week change +0.07 and 13-week change +0.17. Every single one of the last 13 readings is higher than the prior one (monotonic tightening), and range_position is at 1.00 — the current reading sits at the top of its 52-week range, right at the 52-week high. This is a steady, low-volatility grind toward tighter conditions, not a one-off spike. Momentum has been consistent, though the pace per period is modest and roughly stable to slightly decelerating (0.02, 0.02, 0.01, 0.02, 0.03, 0.02, 0.02, 0.02, 0.02, 0.01, 0.01, 0.02 per step). There is no sign of reversal in the data. Even though the index remains negative (-0.28), meaning conditions are still looser than the historical average, the trajectory is unambiguously toward tightening, and being at the 52-week high with continued positive short-term momentum suggests the move continues rather than stalls. Absent any measurement showing deceleration to zero or reversal, the base case is a continuation of the grind higher into month-end.
Would change its mind if: A flattening or reversal in the weekly NFCI prints, or nfci_change_4w turning negative or near zero, would signal the tightening trend has stalled.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_high

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last four prints (1.34, 1.36, 1.40, 1.42, 1.41) show breakevens grinding higher off the recent trough of 1.28, even though the 21d and 63d changes remain negative on net because the window still captures the earlier decline. The most recent three-month trajectory is clearly a recovery: from 1.32/1.28 lows up to 1.40-1.42, with the latest reading only a touch off the highest recent print. My prior call anticipating further softness looks wrong given this rebound — the series has been building upward momentum, not eroding. Breakevens sit in the lower fifth of their 12-month range (range_position 0.20) and remain 0.59pp below the 2% objective, so there's little sign of expectations becoming unanchored to the upside, but the short-term slope is now positive and consistent for four consecutive readings. Policy language describing "market-based measures of inflation compensation declined further" appears stale relative to the price action shown, reinforcing that the language lags the recent uptick. Given the consistent grind higher over the last several observations and no reversal in the newest print, I lean toward continued modest firming into month-end, though this is a low-conviction lean given proximity to a plateau (1.42→1.41) that could just as easily flatten out.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back below 1.36-1.38 over the next few prints, or renewed decline in be_change_21d, would negate the recovery thesis.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Headline CPI y/y has surged from near-zero to 1.24% over the last three prints, with the last two m/m readings (-0.11, -0.05) puzzling against that acceleration — the y/y jump is being driven by base effects (weak prints rolling off from a year ago) rather than fresh momentum. Indeed headline_3m_annualized is -0.14%, essentially flat-to-falling on a recent-months basis, and headline_mom_3m_avg is -0.01%, both showing outgoing momentum is weaker than what's pushing the y/y rate up. This tells me the y/y acceleration is mostly a base-effect artifact of comparing against last year's energy-driven declines, and my prior call (if it anticipated continued acceleration) was directionally right on the print itself but the underlying momentum has already stalled. Core PCE remains the more telling read on persistence: it's basically flat at 1.19-1.20%, still 0.81pp below the 2% target, with no meaningful move in three months (core_yoy_change_3m -0.04pp). Core PCE shows no acceleration at all. Given headline's recent monthly prints are negative/flat and base effects from a year ago are now largely in the y/y number already, the room for further mechanical y/y acceleration is narrowing unless energy turns up again. I lean toward flat-to-slightly-down on headline next print, with low-moderate conviction given base-effect uncertainty.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in energy prices or a strong positive headline m/m print (>0.2%) would signal renewed upward momentum, not just base effects.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, headline_mom_3m_avg, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, core_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate just fell to 4.80% from 5.00%, a sharp 0.20pp one-month drop that pushed the series to a fresh 12-month low. Over 3/6/12 months the trend is unambiguously down (-0.20, -0.40, -0.90 pp respectively), and the rate now sits at the bottom of its 12-month range (range position 0.0, above-low gap 0.0). The Sahm-style gap (3m avg vs 12m low) is a modest +0.17pp, well below the ~0.5pp threshold that would signal a turning point toward recession-like loosening. Policy language corroborates continued tightening: "ongoing job gains and declining unemployment," "underutilization of labor resources has diminished appreciably," and "considerable improvement in labor market conditions this year." There is no evidence of a reversal in trend — the single latest print is the strongest tightening move in the series, not an aberration against the grain. My prior view (if it anticipated continued decline) is validated by this print. Given the level is still 0.8pp above the natural rate, there's room for further tightening, and momentum is clearly downward with no signs of stalling. I expect the unemployment rate to continue drifting lower or at worst hold near this new low next month, given the strength and consistency of the recent moves.
Would change its mind if: A print back up to 5.0%+ or a Sahm gap rising above ~0.3-0.5pp would indicate the tightening trend has reversed into loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year yield has drifted down from ~2.03-2.07% range to 1.94% at the latest print, with the 21-day change at -0.38pp — a meaningful decline — while the 63-day change is only -0.11pp, suggesting most of the drop is recent and possibly accelerating. The 252-day change is still +0.21pp, so the longer trend has been up, but the near-term momentum has clearly reversed lower. The yield sits at 0.32 on the 12-month range (32nd percentile), closer to the low end, indicating the long end is pricing more toward flight-to-quality/growth-concern territory than toward richer term premium. The 30s10s slope at 0.81pp is fairly steep, which could reflect long-end anchoring relative to the 10-year, but doesn't override the recent downward move in the level itself. Policy language describes an accommodative Fed maintaining reinvestment of maturing securities and MBS proceeds, keeping longer-term security holdings sizable — this caps upward pressure on term premium from supply/runoff, and using a soft language on inflation expectations (market-based measures declined) that would not support higher yields. Given the sharp recent decline, low range position, and dovish/accommodative context with no runoff pressure mentioned, I lean toward continued softness or at least no reversal higher into month end.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in dgs10_level above ~2.05% with 21d change turning positive would signal the decline has reversed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  inflation_expectations lacks inflation — Realized CPI trend would clarify whether breakeven uptick reflects genuine repricing or noise.
  inflation_expectations lacks term_premium — Rising breakevens could reflect term premium shifts rather than pure inflation expectations.
  inflation lacks labor_tightness — Tighter labor markets could signal building wage-driven inflation pressure ahead of core PCE turning up.
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Confirm whether reinvestment/runoff policy is still suppressing long-end supply pressure or shifting.
  term_premium lacks risk_appetite — Gauge whether the recent yield decline reflects flight-to-quality that could persist or reverse.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (4,489,796) is up from the prior reading (4,483,629) and the last several weeks show noisy but net-positive movement: 4w change +7,447M, 13w change +12,708M, and 13w avg pct change +0.28%. This confirms my prior "flat" call was reasonably close but slightly understates the modest upward drift — assets are oscillating in a tight band around 4.48-4.50T with no clear trend break, consistent with the stated policy of reinvesting maturing Treasuries and MBS paydowns to keep holdings at "sizable levels." There is no language here about tapering reinvestment or announcing a runoff cap; the statement explicitly commits to continuing reinvestment "until normalization... is well under way." Assets remain just below the 52w high (-14,908M), and the 52w change is small (+3,071M), reinforcing a picture of a balance sheet on a plateau with small week-to-week noise rather than directional expansion or contraction. Given the reinvestment policy is unchanged and the recent 4w/13w changes are positive but small relative to the ~$4.5T base (roughly 0.1-0.3%), I read this as continued flat/plateau behavior, with a slight upward tilt from noise-level reinvestment flows rather than active QE.
Would change its mind if: A statement announcing tapering/ending reinvestment or an explicit runoff cap, or several consecutive weeks of declines >$20-30B/month, would flip this to down.

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up/tightening, conviction 0.55) has not played out: the last four weekly prints are -0.28, -0.28, -0.27, -0.27, -0.28 — essentially flat, and nfci_change_4w is +0.00. The tightening trend that drove the 13-week rise (+0.17 from the 13w average of -0.33) appears to have stalled over the most recent month. The index sits near the top of its 52-week range (range_position 0.96) and just off the 52-week high, but momentum has clearly decelerated to zero. Level remains well below zero (-0.28), so conditions are still looser than historical average, but the direction of travel that matters for the one-month-ahead call is the near-term momentum, which is flat. There's no fresh policy language suggesting a shift in accommodation stance beyond the standard gradual-normalization boilerplate, so I don't see a catalyst for renewed tightening or a reversal into easing. Given the plateau in the last five readings, the most defensible call is flat, with modest conviction — the multi-month uptrend could resume, but current evidence shows stalling, not continuation.
Would change its mind if: A resumption of upward weekly prints (e.g., two consecutive increases) or nfci_change_4w turning clearly positive would revive the tightening call.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year breakeven has continued its recovery, printing a fresh series high at 1.42 from 1.41, extending a steady uptrend over the last several observations (1.24→1.42). My prior "up" call at 0.35 conviction was correct in direction and the trend has persisted rather than reversed, so I hold the call. However, be_change_21d is flat at +0.00pp, meaning the most recent month has stalled even as the longer trend (63d and especially the multi-print run) remains positive — momentum is decelerating. Breakevens sit at 1.42, still well below the 12m high of 1.94 and 0.58pp under the 2% objective, with range_position at 0.32 indicating breakevens are in the lower third of their 12-month range despite the recent bounce. The policy language (stale, describing breakevens as "declined further" with survey measures "little changed") is backward-looking and does not reflect the subsequent recovery, so I discount it heavily as it predates the current print sequence. Given the flattening 21-day change alongside continued distance from target and the low position in the 12-month range, I see the uptrend as intact but losing steam — a moderate rather than strong case for further gains next month.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 1.39-1.40 or renewed negative be_change_21d over the next few prints would negate the recovery thesis.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target, be_12m_high

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 14 days ago.
Report:
  Last release confirmed a pullback: headline_mom came in at -0.13%, extending the softening from -0.11% and -0.05% the prior two months—my "flat" call at 0.35 conviction was too neutral given this; the actual print showed deceleration in month/month terms even as the yoy figure dropped from 1.24 to 0.85, a sizeable -0.39pp swing. That yoy decline was driven by base effects rolling off a strong prior-year comparison rather than a genuine trend reversal, but the 3-month annualized headline is now -1.14%, clearly negative and the weakest momentum reading in the series. Core PCE ticked up to 1.41 from a 1.18-1.23 range, its highest print in the window, which is the one data point arguing for firming underlying pressure. But headline is what's being asked about, and headline's recent trajectory (three straight negative-to-flat mom prints, averaging -0.10%) points toward either a flat-to-lower yoy reading next month absent an energy rebound. The prior-year comparison base looks less favorable now (yoy is off its 1.24 high), meaning further base-effect declines are less likely, but recent mom weakness dominates the near-term signal. Net: momentum has clearly turned down even as core inflation firmed—a divergence I weight toward headline's own momentum for this call.
Would change its mind if: A strong positive headline m/m print (>0.2%), e.g. from energy rebound, would reverse this deceleration signal and imply renewed upward yoy momentum.
Leaned on: headline_mom, headline_cpi_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 21 days ago.
Report:
  The prior call was "down" (further tightening) and the new print came in at 4.90%, down from 5.00% — so that call was directionally correct despite the noisy path (5.00→4.80→4.90). The unemployment rate remains in a clear downtrend on all horizons: -0.2pp over 3m, -0.2pp over 6m, -0.6pp over 12m, sitting at the 12m low region (4.8-4.9% vs 12m low of 4.80%). The Sahm gap is a tiny +0.10pp, far below the ~0.3-0.5pp threshold that would signal a regime shift into loosening. Range position is just 0.13 (near the bottom of the 12m range), and the rate sits 0.9pp below the ~4% natural-rate anchor is not quite right — actually it's roughly at/near natural rate territory now (4.9% vs 4% "natural" reference implies the gap framing given is unusual, but level-wise the market is tight by historical standards for this series' own range). The one-month bounce from 4.80 to 4.90 is worth noting — a small uptick after a fresh low — but one month of noise is not sufficient to overturn a multi-month tightening trend, especially with policy language describing continued labor market improvement. I lean toward continued modest tightening or flat, not a reversal to sustained loosening, but I temper conviction given the recent uptick could be early evidence of stabilization rather than pure noise.
Would change its mind if: A print at or above 5.0% next release, or Sahm gap rising above ~0.3-0.5pp, would signal the tightening trend has reversed into loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_12m_low

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct: dgs10_level has continued to grind lower, closing the last print at 1.74% versus prior observations near 1.78-1.81%. The 21d (-0.26pp), 63d (-0.49pp), and 252d (-0.29pp) changes are all negative, confirming sustained downward momentum in the long end rather than a one-off dip. The yield sits at 0.13 on the 12-month range (just 0.11pp above the 12m low of 1.63%, far below the 2.50% high), indicating the term premium is compressed near cycle lows, consistent with flight-to-quality or duration demand rather than issuance-driven cheapening. The 10s30s slope at 0.87pp is moderately steep but has not been rising in a way that signals reflation or issuance concerns dominating; the level and momentum evidence dominate the read. Policy language reflects an accommodative stance with gradual tightening expected and soft inflation compensation, which does not point to upward pressure on long-end premia. Given the yield is pinned near lows with negative momentum across all horizons and no sign of the 2.05% reversal threshold I set previously, I maintain a down view, though conviction is tempered since the recent 21d change has decelerated somewhat (last few prints are choppy between 1.71-1.76) rather than accelerating further.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in dgs10_level above ~2.00% with 21d change turning positive would signal the decline has reversed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace of runoff/reinvestment changes would clarify supply pressure on long-end premium.
  term_premium lacks risk_appetite — Flight-to-quality flows would help distinguish safe-haven demand from other drivers of the low yield.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call holds up. Total assets are essentially flat-to-fluctuating around ~$4.48-4.50T with no discernible trend: the last observation (4,492,857) sits close to mid-range of the 13-week window, and 4w change is a small +$3.06B while 13w change is a small -$3.67B (-0.08%). These are noise-level moves consistent with normal reinvestment operations (rolling over Treasuries at auction, reinvesting MBS paydowns into MBS), not a policy shift. The 52w change is also tiny (+$12.25B on a ~$4.5T base). The policy language confirms this: the Committee explicitly states it is maintaining existing reinvestment policy for agency debt/MBS and rolling over maturing Treasuries at auction "until normalization of the level of the federal funds rate is well under way" — meaning holdings are being kept at sizable, stable levels by design. There is no mention of tapering, runoff, or balance sheet reduction; the framework is still rate-normalization-first, balance-sheet-stable. Level is $11.8B below the 52w high, an immaterial gap. With reinvestment intact and full auction rollover continuing, next month's total assets should stay in this same tight band — modest week-to-week noise around a flat trend, not a sustained expansion or contraction.
Would change its mind if: A statement announcing tapering/ending reinvestment or an explicit runoff cap, or sustained declines exceeding $20-30B/month, would flip this to down.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_below_52w_high

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The series has clearly reversed from its mid-series peak. After bottoming near -0.35 and rising to -0.27 (tightening), NFCI has now turned back down for four consecutive prints (-0.27→-0.28→-0.30→-0.32→-0.35), landing back at the series low. nfci_change_4w is -0.07 (clear easing momentum), while nfci_change_13w is only +0.02, showing the longer trend is flat/noisy but the recent trajectory is decisively down. nfci_range_position at 0.71 is stale relative to the newest print, which is actually at the 52w low, not near the high — the range stats lag the last two observations. My prior flat call was wrong: I was waiting for confirmation of renewed tightening, but instead conditions eased sharply, erasing the entire prior uptrend. With four straight weekly declines and a clearly negative 4-week change, momentum strongly favors continued easing or at least a level well below zero at month end. Distance from zero remains firmly negative (-0.35), reinforcing loose conditions. Absent any offsetting policy signal (the FOMC language shown is old/generic and doesn't indicate a shift), I now expect further modest easing or at minimum a continuation of the current easing bias into month end.
Would change its mind if: Two consecutive weekly upticks in nfci or nfci_change_4w turning positive would signal the easing trend has reversed back toward tightening.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_change_13w

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (1.62) pulled back from the recent local peak of 1.65 but remains well above the series lows and above the prior print sequence's mid-range. The last observation shows -0.03 from the immediate prior print, but the broader trend remains constructive: be_change_21d is +0.14pp and be_change_63d is +0.09pp, both positive, indicating breakevens have risen over the past month and quarter even after the small pullback. The longer be_change_252d is still -0.11pp, so on a full-year view expectations are lower, but the more recent momentum dominates for a one-month-ahead call. be_range_position at 0.58 shows breakevens sitting modestly above the middle of their 12-month range, not at an extreme, and be_distance_from_target (-0.38pp) shows breakevens still running below the 2% objective, consistent with policy language describing inflation compensation as "low" and expectations "little changed." My prior up call was directionally validated as the series climbed from 1.57 to a peak of 1.65 before the modest give-back to 1.62. The pullback is minor relative to the run-up, and the 21d/63d momentum readings still point up. I'll maintain a modest up lean, tempered by the recent two-print softening and the still-below-target level.
Would change its mind if: A break back below ~1.56-1.57 (recent trough) or negative be_change_21d in the next print would negate this modest up lean.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior call (down, 0.45) was wrong: headline y/y rose again to 0.89% from 0.85%, and the m/m print rebounded to +0.31%, exactly the kind of energy-led bounce I flagged as my falsifier. Headline y/y has climbed from -0.10% to 0.89% over the trailing year, with yoy_range_position at 0.74 showing we're in the upper part of the 12-month range, and headline_3m and yoy_change_3m still positive though headline_3m_annualized (+0.54%) is actually below the latest single-month pace, suggesting the m/m series is choppy rather than smoothly accelerating. Both series show synchronized upward momentum after a long soft patch, which is a more convincing signal than headline alone. user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call is confirmed by the latest data. Total assets fell to 8,890,004 from 8,899,213, continuing the runoff trend. The 4-week change is -23,549M and the 13-week change is -49,195M, both negative, consistent with ongoing QT. Assets are now 75,483M below the 52-week high and well below the 13-week average (8,917,901), indicating the decline is not a blip but a sustained trend. The path since the recent local peak (~8,945,898) has been choppy week-to-week (a few small upticks) but the net trajectory is clearly downward, consistent with the FOMC statement's explicit language that the Committee "will continue reducing its holdings of Treasury securities and agency debt and agency mortgage-backed securities" per the announced caps. There is no language suggesting a pause, taper, or end to runoff — the statement reaffirms continuation of the balance sheet reduction plan alongside rate hikes. Given the steady pace of decline over the past month and quarter, and no policy signal of a change, I expect total assets to be lower again at next month-end. The magnitude of monthly decline (~20-25k in recent weeks) suggests continued modest runoff pace.
Would change its mind if: A multi-week rise in fed_assets back above ~8,930,000 combined with statement language announcing pause or end of runoff caps would flip me to up.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high, assets_13w_avg

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (up/tightening, conviction 0.65) has been falsified. Since that call, the NFCI printed three consecutive declines (-0.17 → -0.18 → -0.20), moving back below the -0.17 local high and further from the 52w high of -0.16. The 4-week change is now -0.03 (easing), even though the 13-week change remains +0.15 (net tightening over the quarter). The series shows a clear tightening trend from -0.34 up to a peak near -0.16, but the last three readings show a stall/reversal exactly matching my own falsifier condition. Conditions remain historically loose (distance from zero -0.20, range position 0.93 — near the loose end of its own 52-week range in absolute terms but still tight relative to its own recent history). Given the recent 3-week downtick, momentum has turned mildly negative even though the level is still historically accommodative and prior direction was toward tightening. This is a genuine trend inflection risk, not a clear continuation, so I lower conviction and lean toward continued easing/flat rather than resuming the tightening call.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back above -0.17 with two consecutive rising prints would restore the tightening trend view.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (down, 0.55) has been invalidated. The last two readings jumped to 2.48 and 2.53, breaking above the 2.50 threshold I set as my falsifier, and be_change_21d has flipped to +0.17pp from what was a declining trend. The 63-day change (-0.44pp) still reflects the earlier drop, but that is now stale relative to the sharp recent reversal — the last four prints (2.36, 2.36, 2.42, 2.48, 2.53) show a clear, accelerating upward move. Breakevens are now +0.53pp above the 2% target, near the upper third of the 12-month range (range_position 0.35, up from the low). This is a fresh, monotonic uptrend over the last three readings, not noise. Policy language remains unchanged (elevated inflation framing, committed to 2% objective), so there's no new signal from that side to explain the move, but the price action itself is what I'm graded on. Given the strength and persistence of the recent climb, and that it has now cleared my own reversal threshold, I'm flipping to up with moderate-to-strong conviction. I'd want to see the move stall or reverse for a couple of readings before doubting it further.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back below 2.45 sustained across 2+ readings, or be_change_21d turning negative, would signal the uptrend has stalled.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target, be_above_12m_low

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print shows a meaningful break: headline CPI y/y fell to 8.46% from 8.98%, and the outgoing m/m reading was -0.01%, essentially flat/slightly negative — a sharp deceleration from the 0.6-1.3% monthly pace seen for most of the prior year. My previous "up" call at 0.60 conviction was wrong; the [date] -[date] acceleration did not extend into this print, and headline actually reversed. Core PCE y/y also ticked up slightly to 5.32% from 5.18%, but this follows two consecutive months of decline (5.60→5.59→5.35→5.18) before this small uptick, so the core trend is still best read as stalling/rolling over rather than reaccelerating — core_yoy_change_3m is -0.28pp over the quarter. The 3m annualized headline pace of 9.10% is stale, driven by the volatile mid-year spike months rolling off; the single-month behavior (-0.01% m/m) is the more telling signal for near-term momentum. Energy/gasoline swings likely explain much of the headline m/m collapse, but that is exactly the kind of base effect that can persist for a print or two given how large a role energy played in the run-up. Given the outgoing_mom near zero and core roughly flat at an elevated level, next month's headline y/y is more likely to decelerate further or hold near current levels than resume climbing toward new highs.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in headline m/m back above 0.6% alongside core PCE y/y climbing above 5.5% would signal reacceleration, not deceleration.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, outgoing_mom, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_yoy_change_3m, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate printed 3.50%, a fresh 12-month low, down from 3.60% the prior two months. My previous "flat" call at 0.40 conviction was wrong in direction of surprise — rather than plateauing at 3.6%, the rate ticked down further, extending the tightening trend rather than stalling. The 3m change (-0.20pp) and 6m change (-0.50pp) confirm continued gradual tightening, though the pace has decelerated sharply versus the 12m change (-1.90pp), which reflects a much steeper decline earlier in the window. Sahm_gap is near zero (+0.07pp) and unrate_above_12m_low is 0.00, meaning there is no evidence of any rise off the recent low — the loosening/recession-signal condition is entirely absent. The level itself (3.50%) is 0.50pp below the natural-rate anchor of ~4%, indicating a historically tight labor market with no current sign of turning. Given the string of readings at 3.6/3.6/3.5, the market is either bottoming or still marginally tightening. Absent any measurement showing acceleration in job losses or a reversal, the most defensible call for the next print is flat-to-slightly-down, but given three consecutive prints at or above the floor with the latest actually setting a new low, I lean toward continued flat/tight conditions rather than a reversal upward.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.7% would confirm the plateau is breaking toward loosening; another 3.5% or lower print would confirm continued tightening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_above_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call has been overtaken by the data: yields have continued sliding, closing the last print at 2.67%, down from 2.78-2.81 in the prior few observations. The 21-day change is -0.43pp, a clear downward move, though the 63-day change of only -0.18pp shows the decline is recent and accelerating rather than a longer grind. Over the past year yields are still up sharply (+1.42pp), and the level sits at 0.64 of the 12-month range - comfortably above the low, so this is a pullback within an uptrend, not a collapse. However, the falsifier I set previously (sustained break below 2.90 with slope_30s10s negative) has partly triggered: yields are well below 2.90, though the 30s10s slope remains positive at +0.33pp, so the long-end curve isn't inverting, just softening. Ten-year yields at the long end look to be drifting lower as growth data mentioned in policy language ("recent indicators of spending and production have softened") likely feeds flight-to-quality/duration demand even as the Fed continues balance-sheet runoff and hikes. Given the clear, recent, and accelerating downward momentum, I shift from flat to a modest down call for month-end, though the still-positive 30s10s slope and elevated range position temper conviction.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above 2.85 with slope_30s10s rising further would signal the downtrend has reversed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

  term_premium lacks curve_slope — Full 2s10s and 5s30s context would clarify if long-end softening reflects broader curve flattening or just term-premium compression.
  term_premium lacks risk_appetite — Would confirm whether the yield decline is flight-to-quality driven, reinforcing the downward call.

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (further easing/down) was wrong: NFCI has now printed three consecutive increases (-0.22, -0.23, -0.24 read oldest-to-newest with the last two flat), reversing the mid-series easing trough of -0.17. Looking at the full 13-week path, conditions eased steadily from -0.26 to a peak looseness of -0.17, then began tightening back toward -0.24 over the last five prints. The 4-week change is now +... wait, actually nfci_change_4w is given as -0.04, indicating net easing over that specific 4-week window, while nfci_change_13w is +0.04, indicating net tightening over the full 13-week window. The two horizons disagree, but the most recent two prints (-0.23, -0.24) are flat-to-slightly-tighter, suggesting the tightening impulse from the trough has stalled rather than reversed further. The index remains deeply negative (-0.24), far below the historical zero average and near the bottom of its 52-week range (range_position 0.85 toward the loose end, low -0.65 to high -0.16), so conditions overall remain quite accommodative despite the recent modest firming. Call: down, conviction 0.45

Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:

This is a fairly clean signal that the prior uptrend has stalled and reversed — my falsifier condition (21d change turning negative / sustained break below 2.45) is close to being met, with the trajectory pointing that direction even if not yet fully triggered. Distance from the 2% target remains meaningful (+0.48pp), so inflation expectations are still elevated versus objective, but the marginal direction over the last three prints is clearly downward. I'm revising to a "down" call for the next month, given the consistent three-print decline and negative 63-day change, though conviction is moderate given the flat 21d reading and still-positive 252d trend leave some ambiguity.
Would change its mind if: A bounce back above 2.55 sustained across 2+ readings, or be_change_21d turning solidly positive, would signal the decline has stalled.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The previous "down" call is validated by the latest print: headline CPI y/y fell to 8.22% from 8.46%, and the outgoing m/m reading was just 0.06%, the softest in the entire 13-month series, following -0.01% the month prior. Two consecutive near-zero m/m prints is a meaningful deceleration signal — headline_mom_3m_avg (0.43%) is now being pulled down sharply by these last two months, and headline_3m_annualized (5.32%) is well below the y/y level of 8.22%, indicating the trend rate has decisively cooled versus the stock. Core PCE y/y also ticked down to 5.11% from 5.32%, continuing a gentle downtrend from its 5.60% peak, though it remains far above target (core_distance_from_target +3.11pp) and is stickier than headline. The core_minus_headline gap (-3.12pp) reflects energy's outsized role in the recent headline plunge, which raises some risk of base-effect payback if energy prices stabilize or rebound. Still, two straight near-flat m/m readings are hard to dismiss, and both headline and core moved down together this time, unlike prior months where they diverged. This is a clear deceleration signal, though the elevated level keeps this from being a high-conviction call given one-month energy swings can reverse quickly.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m rebound above 0.6% combined with core PCE y/y climbing back above 5.4% would signal reacceleration, not continued deceleration.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, outgoing_mom

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print ticked up to 3.60% from the 3.50% low, a one-tick increase that keeps the series within the tight plateau it has occupied for roughly a year. My prior "flat" call was essentially correct in spirit: the print landed inside the range I was watching (not a clean break above 3.7%, nor a fresh low at or below 3.5%). The uptick to 3.6% is minor and could easily be noise rather than a trend change. Looking at the broader picture: unrate_change_3m is flat at 0.00pp, unrate_change_6m is still modestly negative (-0.30pp), and unrate_change_12m shows a sizable -1.50pp decline — all consistent with a labor market that has been tightening or at worst stabilizing at a low level, not loosening. The sahm_gap remains tiny (+0.07pp), nowhere near the 0.5pp threshold that would signal a genuine turning point. distance_from_natural is -0.40pp, meaning the rate remains below the 4% natural-rate benchmark, i.e., still a tight labor market by level. The single-tick uptick is the only mild loosening signal, but it's too small against three months of flat readings and a year of decline to call a clear direction change. I lean toward flat, with a slight tilt toward up given the tick and the fact that we're bouncing off the cycle low, but conviction remains modest.
Would change its mind if: A print of 3.8% or higher would confirm a genuine loosening trend; a return to 3.5% or below would confirm continued tightening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_range_position

The 10-year is now at the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.85, only 0.34pp below the 12m high of 3.49), and momentum is positive across all horizons: +0.40pp over 21d, +0.21pp over 63d, and a large +1.84pp over 252d. The last three prints (3.04, 3.12, 3.11, 3.15) show steady upward drift with no reversal. The 30s10s slope is modestly positive (+0.12pp), consistent with continued long-end cheapening/curve steepening at the back end, which typically accompanies rising term premium from balance-sheet runoff and issuance concerns — reinforced here by the explicit policy language confirming ongoing balance-sheet reduction. There's no flight-to-quality signal evident. Given the sustained uptrend, proximity to 12-month highs, and steepening 30s10s, I now judge the term premium is rising, not falling. Conviction is moderate-clear given the consistent multi-horizon momentum, tempered by the fact the move has been grinding rather than accelerating.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below ~2.95 with 30s10s slope flattening or inverting would signal the uptrend has stalled or reversed.

With QT still active per the statement and the trailing 4-week and 13-week changes both solidly negative, the pace looks steady rather than accelerating or decelerating. Call: down, conviction 0.55

The 21d (-0.03pp) and 63d (-0.08pp) changes both point modestly lower, while the 252d change is still positive (+0.07pp), so the longer-term trend remains mildly up even as short-term momentum has faded and stabilized. Breakeven sits comfortably mid-range (range_position 0.61) and 0.29pp above the 2% target, not near either 12-month extreme, arguing against a directional breakout in either direction. === inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  Last release invalidated my "down" call: headline CPI y/y jumped to 2.68% from 2.38%, and the m/m print came in at 0.25%, above the prior three-month average and consistent with reacceleration rather than continued disinflation. The 3m annualized rate for both headline and core sits at 2.08%, which is actually consistent with target, but the trend in the last two prints (0.16% then 0.25% m/m) is accelerating versus the 0.03-0.16% readings seen two-three months prior. Core PCE also ticked back up to 2.78% from a recent low of 2.61%, reversing three months of gradual cooling. Headline y/y is now mid-range within its 12-month band (0.53 range position) rather than pinned at the low, and the y/y change over 3 months has flipped positive (+0.30pp) even as the core y/y change over 3 months is still slightly negative (-0.18pp) — a divergence, but I weight the recent core PCE uptick and the headline momentum jump together since both moved in the same direction this month. This looks like a stalling-out or modest reacceleration of the disinflation trend rather than a clean continuation of cooling, though one month's core PCE increase could be noise. I lean toward a further modest rise in headline next month.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print back below 0.15% with core PCE resuming its decline toward 2.6% would confirm disinflation has resumed.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, yoy_range_position

That confirms the 4.30% print was noise within a tight 4.00-4.30% range rather than a genuine trend break. Looking at the full series, the unemployment rate has oscillated in a narrow 0.30pp band for 13 months with no directional drift: unrate_change_6m and unrate_change_12m are both 0.00pp, and the 3m change is now -0.10pp, pulling back toward the low end. The Sahm-gap of 0.20pp is well below the 0.50pp threshold that would signal genuine labor-market deterioration, and the level itself (4.10%) sits only modestly above the ~4.00% natural-rate anchor. Policy language describes conditions as "stabilized at a low level" and "solid," consistent with a flat, range-bound labor market rather than one that is tightening or loosening in a sustained way. Given the mean-reverting pattern and the immediate reversal of last month's uptick, I expect the unemployment rate to hold near 4.10-4.20% next month rather than move decisively in either direction.
Would change its mind if: A move back to or above 4.30%, or two consecutive months rising above 4.20%, would signal renewed loosening and change this view.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_range_width, unrate_12m_low, unrate_12m_high

Range position at 0.53 is mid-range, so there's no stretched extreme argument either way. The 30s10s slope at +0.54pp is little changed and doesn't show the steepening that would signal an acceleration of long-end-specific selling; it's roughly stable, consistent with a broad-based richening rather than one confined to the very long end. Absent a stated catalyst in the policy language (unchanged FOMC statement, still discussing balance sheet runoff), the move looks like it's coming from outside the Fed's stated stance — likely flight-to-quality or growth concerns feeding into duration demand. Given the clear, recent, multi-print downward trajectory, I lean toward continued softness into month-end, though the shallow 63-day change tempers conviction.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back above 4.35 with steepening 30s10s would signal the decline has stalled; a push above 4.55 would confirm renewed term-premium rise.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call was wrong: the latest data show continued, indeed accelerating, declines. Assets fell from 6,580,462 to 6,552,419 over the last observation (a ~34,615 4-week drop), and the 13-week change is now -50,965, with assets sitting 345,066 below the 52-week high. This is consistent runoff, not stabilization. However, the statement explicitly says the Committee "decided to conclude the reduction of its aggregate securities holdings" as of a stated date - i.e., QT is being announced as ending. The measurement series still shows declines through the most recent print, which likely reflects the lag between the announcement and actual halt of runoff (redemptions already in the pipeline, or the conclusion date falling after the last observation). Given the announced end of balance sheet reduction, the pace of decline should slow sharply and then flatten in the coming month as reinvestment resumes. But since the most recent weekly prints still show a fresh acceleration in the drawdown (-34,615 over 4 weeks, largest recent single-step -22,864 to -2,864 last two prints), there's a real chance the tail end of runoff continues to shave assets modestly before fully halting. I'll lean toward flat-to-slightly-down for month-end, with reduced conviction given the transition.
Would change its mind if: A weekly print showing assets flat or rising post-conclusion date would confirm the halt; continued declines beyond the stated conclusion date would falsify it.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has been essentially flat at very loose levels for the observed window, drifting from -0.52 to -0.50 — a marginal +0.01 to +0.02 index uptick over both 4-week and 13-week windows. This is a slight tightening in direction but trivially small in magnitude, well within noise for an index whose 52-week range is only -0.52 to -0.38. My prior flat call was correct: conditions did not break through either the -0.53 or -0.49 thresholds I set, and the index continues to sit deep in negative (loose) territory, near its 52-week low (range position 0.14). The tiny uptick over the last few prints (-0.51 to -0.50) could be early momentum toward tightening, but it is far too small and short-lived to call a trend change. Fed policy language shows a cut in the funds rate with continued easing bias, consistent with financial conditions staying loose rather than tightening meaningfully. Absent a sharper move or a break of the range, the most defensible call remains flat, though I'll acknowledge the slight upward drift keeps a mild tightening lean alive for next month.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move below -0.53 (easing) or above -0.48 (tightening) holding for two consecutive weeks would overturn this flat call.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_low, nfci_52w_high

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call has been overtaken by the data. Breakeven has drifted down from 2.28 to 2.23 over the shown window, with be_change_21d -0.05pp and be_change_63d -0.20pp confirming a steady, not noisy, decline. The last four readings (2.24, 2.23, 2.22, 2.23) show the decline decelerating and possibly stabilizing near 2.22-2.23, but there's no sign of a bounce back toward the 2.28 starting point. be_range_position of 0.21 shows breakeven sitting in the lower fifth of its 12-month range, and it's now only 0.06pp above the 12-month low, having fallen from a high of 2.46. Distance from the 2% target remains positive at +0.23pp, so expectations are still anchored above target, but the trend of the last one-to-three months is clearly downward. Given the my prior threshold (sustained move below 2.26 for two consecutive releases), that condition is now met — the last four prints are all below 2.26. This warrants revising to a "down" lean, though conviction is moderate since the last two prints (2.22, 2.23) suggest the pace of decline is flattening out near current levels rather than accelerating further.
Would change its mind if: A move back above 2.26 sustained for two consecutive releases, or renewed upward acceleration in be_change_21d, would invalidate the down call.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.60) was correct: headline CPI y/y rose from 3.02 to 2.99... wait, actually it ticked down slightly to 2.99 from 3.02, but that's essentially flat/marginal decline after a run-up from 2.68→2.74→2.94→3.02→2.99. The last print shows the acceleration stalling. Headline m/m of 0.25% is in line with the 3m average (0.30%) and slightly below the outgoing print (0.28%), suggesting momentum is plateauing rather than re-accelerating or reversing. Headline 3m annualized (3.64%) remains well above target and above the y/y rate, indicating recent momentum is still hot even as the y/y number itself has stopped climbing. Core PCE continues to soften on trend (2.91→2.83→2.75), with core_yoy_change_3m at -0.11pp, reinforcing that underlying persistence is easing modestly even though core remains 0.75pp above target. Headline sits near the top of its 12-month range (yoy_range_position 0.95), and the small dip from 3.02 to 2.99 could be noise rather than a turn. Given the plateau in momentum and the slight pullback, I lean toward a flat to slightly softer headline print next month, not a continued acceleration, but conviction is limited since 3m annualized momentum is still running above the y/y rate, which argues against a large drop.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.30% or a renewed jump in core PCE would signal reacceleration and reverse this call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, yoy_range_position, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.60
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The most recent print rose to 4.50% from 4.40%, confirming the prior "up" call. The trend over the past several months shows a clear, persistent drift higher: 3m change +0.20pp, 6m change +0.30pp, 12m change +0.40pp, with the series now sitting at its 12-month high and at the top of its range (range_position = 1.00). The unemployment rate has moved from a 4.00% trough to 4.50%, a 0.50pp rise that is approaching Sahm-rule-adjacent territory (sahm_gap +0.40pp), and the level is now 0.50pp above the ~4.0% natural-rate anchor. Critically, the print did not revert to 4.20% or below as would have been needed to call the prior rise noise — instead it accelerated further, making a new cycle high. Policy language corroborates this, explicitly noting job gains have slowed, unemployment has "edged up," and downside risks to employment have risen enough to prompt a rate cut. The combination of a monotonic-ish uptrend, a fresh high, and consistent policy commentary describing loosening labor conditions gives a fairly confident signal that the market continues to loosen. I do not see evidence here of stabilization; if anything the pace of increase is picking up slightly (3m pace of +0.20pp is faster than prior periods).
Would change its mind if: A print back at or below 4.30% would suggest the recent acceleration was noise and the market is re-stabilizing rather than continuing to loosen.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_high, unrate_range_position, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous call (up, 0.35) was wrong: dgs10 continued its decline from 4.14 to a low of 4.00 before ticking up modestly to 4.02 in the last print. The 21-day change is now barely positive (+0.03pp) after a sharp 63-day decline of -0.22pp and a 252-day decline of -0.39pp, showing the broader trend remains firmly down even as the most recent two prints show a small bounce off the lows. The yield sits at just 0.05pp above its 12-month low and only 6% into its 12-month range (0.06 range_position), indicating the long end is pinned near the bottom of its year-long band, not showing genuine reversal. The 10s30s slope (+0.65pp) and 10s2s slope (+0.55pp) remain positively sloped but unremarkable, not signaling a supply/issuance-driven steepening that would argue for materially higher long yields. The Fed's announcement of ending balance-sheet runoff (QT conclusion) is a mild source of support for the long end (removes a supply headwind), consistent with the halt in the decline rather than a reversal. Given the tiny 2-day uptick sits within noise against a dominant multi-month downtrend and the yield remains near 12-month lows, I lean toward continued softness or flat, not a resumption of upward term premium pressure.
Would change its mind if: A clean break above 4.20 with sustained steepening would confirm reversal; a fall back below 4.00 would confirm continued decline.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s, slope_10s2s

  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Confirmation of pace/timing of QT conclusion effects on long-end supply/demand dynamics.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print shows total assets rising sharply to 6,581,231 from 6,556,861, and the 4-week change has flipped positive (+28,812 $M) after months of decline. This confirms the prior call was wrong to expect continued runoff into this period — the series bottomed around 6,535,781 and has now risen for two consecutive weeks, the second increase quite large. The 13-week change is still negative (-27,164) and the 52-week change remains deeply negative (-304,732), so on a longer lookback QT is still the dominant regime, but the recent pace has clearly reversed. The policy language confirms the mechanism: the Committee explicitly states reserves have declined to ample levels and it will initiate purchases of shorter-term Treasuries to maintain ample reserves — this is an explicit announcement of balance sheet expansion (organic growth operations), not merely a pause. Given the stated intent to actively purchase securities going forward, and the fact that the most recent two prints already show meaningful increases, I expect total assets to be higher at next month-end. The move is early and could be volatile week to week, but the direction and the stated policy rationale align.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back to weekly declines in fed_assets, or the Fed clarifying purchases are merely offsetting bill maturities rather than net growth.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_change_52w

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series remains extremely stable, oscillating in a narrow -0.52 to -0.50 band for the last 13 weeks with essentially no trend. The 4-week change is a negligible -0.02 and the 13-week change is flat at +0.00, confirming no directional momentum. The index sits well below the zero average (distance -0.52), indicating financial conditions remain looser than historical norms, but that level itself has been stable for months — range_position of 0.01 shows we're pinned near the 52-week low, not moving toward the high. The prior flat call was correct: the latest print (-0.52) is right back within the established micro-range and did not breach either threshold I set (-0.53 or -0.48). Policy language shows the Fed cutting rates 25bp with a split committee (one dissent for larger cut, two for no change), reflecting a cautious, data-dependent stance amid labor market softening — this is consistent with easing bias in monetary policy but has not yet translated into any meaningful loosening or tightening of broad financial conditions per the index itself. With no momentum and the index oscillating within a 0.02-wide band for over a quarter, I see no basis for a directional call over the next month.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move below -0.53 (easing) or above -0.48 (tightening) holding for two consecutive weeks would overturn this flat call.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.20
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print (2.25) ticked up from 2.22-2.24 range, and be_change_21d is now +0.01pp — a mild reversal from the prior modest downtrend. However, be_change_63d and be_change_252d remain firmly negative at -0.10pp each, showing the medium/longer-term trend is still down from recent highs. My previous "down" call at 0.45 conviction is only partially validated: the multi-month decline did occur (confirmed by the -0.10pp readings), but the most recent single observation shows a small bounce, not continuation. Breakevens sit at 2.25%, still 0.25pp above the 2% target, in the upper-middle of the 12-month range (0.28 range position, closer to low than high). The series is essentially flat over the last 10 observations (2.22-2.25 band), with no clear acceleration in either direction. Policy language is unchanged/boilerplate on inflation expectations, offering no new signal. Given the tiny recent uptick against a backdrop of a stalled-but-not-reversing longer-term decline, I see this as a wash — the data doesn't support continuing the down call with the same conviction, nor does it support flipping to up. This looks like consolidation near 2.23-2.25 rather than fresh directional momentum.
Would change its mind if: A break above 2.28 or below 2.20 sustained across two releases, or a sharp move in be_change_21d beyond +/-0.05pp, would clarify direction.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The last print came in at 3.00% y/y headline, essentially flat vs the prior 2.99% — my prior "flat" call was correct, and the m/m data continues to support it: outgoing_mom of 0.34% is right at the 0.30% threshold I flagged, but the 3m average m/m (0.28%) and headline_3m_annualized (3.43%) show momentum still running slightly above the y/y trend, keeping headline sticky rather than falling. Headline yoy_range_position is 0.97 — essentially at the top of its 12-month range — confirming headline has climbed off its mid-year low (2.33%) and stabilized near cycle highs rather than decelerating further. Core PCE, the better persistence gauge, tells a slightly different story: it printed 2.83%, down from 2.91% two prints ago, and core_yoy_change_3m is -0.08pp, a mild disinflationary drift. Core sits 0.83pp above target — still elevated but not accelerating. Weighing both: core's modest cooling argues against a sharp reacceleration, but headline's m/m readings (0.30, 0.25, 0.30) are firm enough to keep the y/y rate pinned near 3% rather than falling. Net: no clear directional trigger — headline looks likely to hold near current levels next month, perhaps ticking a touch higher given firm recent m/m prints against last year's likely base effects, but not with strong conviction either way.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print below 0.15% would signal renewed deceleration; one above 0.40% would confirm reacceleration and flip this to up with higher conviction.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, yoy_range_position, outgoing_mom, headline_yoy_change_3m

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The last print ticked down to 4.40% from 4.50%, following the prior view that called for further loosening at 0.60 conviction. That call was partially right in trend but the pullback to 4.40 from 4.50 is the kind of print my own falsifier flagged as a sign of re-stabilization, though it's above the 4.30 threshold I set, so I won't fully reverse. Looking at the full series: from a 12-month low of 4.00%, the rate has risen 40bp, with the last six readings oscillating in the 4.30-4.50 range rather than showing a clean monotonic climb. The 3m and 6m changes are both a modest +0.10pp, and the 12m change is +0.20pp — no acceleration, in fact deceleration in the recent pace versus the jump from 4.30 to 4.50 that preceded this print. The sahm_gap of 0.43pp is a real signal of cumulative loosening (well off the 12m low), and unrate_range_position at 0.80 shows we're near the top of the past year's range. But the single-month decline suggests the ascent may be leveling off rather than continuing to accelerate. Policy language confirms unemployment "edged up but remained low," consistent with gradual, not runaway, loosening. I lean toward continued mild loosening or flatness rather than a strong new leg down, but the retreat this month tempers confidence in a strong "up" call.
Would change its mind if: A print back at or below 4.30% would confirm re-stabilization; a fresh push to 4.50%+ would confirm renewed loosening acceleration.

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10Y closed the last print at 4.18, matching the recent high in this 10-observation window and sitting within a choppy 4.12-4.18 range. My prior "flat" call holds up reasonably well: yields have oscillated without a clean directional break, and neither of my stated triggers (sustained break above 4.20 with steepening, or a fall below 4.00) has fired. That said, the 21-day change (+0.09pp) and the uptick to 4.18 from a 4.12-4.14 cluster suggest mild upward drift within the range rather than pure noise. The 63-day change is only +0.03pp, confirming this is a shallow drift, not a trend. Range position at 0.26 (near the low end of the 12-month range) means there's room to rise before any technical resistance, and the 30s10s slope at 0.66pp is steep-ish, consistent with modest term-premium building rather than compression. The 252-day change of -0.40pp shows the larger trend is still down, so this looks like consolidation/bottoming with a slight upward bias rather than a reversal. Balance-sheet language noting reserves are "ample" and purchases of shorter-term securities will support ample reserves is mildly long-end supportive (less duration absorption pressure), a modest offset to any upward drift. Net: marginal lean up, low conviction.
Would change its mind if: A clean break below 4.10 with flattening slope would confirm range holding/reversal down; a push above 4.25 would confirm firmer up-trend.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  inflation lacks labor_tightness — Wage growth trends would clarify whether core services inflation persistence is building or fading.
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Details on pace of runoff/QT vs the shorter-term purchase program would clarify duration supply pressure on the long end.
  term_premium lacks positioning — Investor positioning/duration exposure would help judge whether the modest uptick reflects real flow or thin technical moves.
